title: The Outing
weakest_section: 16:00-17:00 range with hospital scene and some transitional moments

jokes.text: I'm tired of pretending I'm excited every time it's somebody's birthday.
jokes.setup: Jerry opens with what seems like a standard birthday complaint
jokes.punchline: The blunt admission of 'pretending' subverts social expectations
jokes.explanation: Strong opening with Jerry's signature observational style. Economy and honesty elevate a common complaint.

jokes.text: All you did was not die for 12 months. That's all you've done, as far as I could tell.
jokes.setup: Jerry questions why we celebrate birthdays repeatedly
jokes.punchline: Reducing life's achievement to merely 'not dying' is darkly reductive
jokes.explanation: Classic Seinfeld - taking a universal experience and finding the absurd through dark logic. Highly quotable and perfectly constructed.

jokes.text: It's like Ed Asner, Elijah Muhammed and Secretariat.
jokes.setup: Jerry mentions astrology's random celebrity birthday groupings
jokes.punchline: The specific combination is absurd - a TV actor, religious leader, and racehorse
jokes.explanation: Good specificity with the mix of human and horse, though relies somewhat on random = funny.

jokes.text: You could throw a dart out the window and hit someone better than me.
jokes.setup: George is having a self-deprecating breakdown
jokes.punchline: The absurd specificity of dart-throwing makes his self-hatred comical
jokes.explanation: Perfect George neurosis with vivid imagery. Character integration is excellent.

jokes.text: Over you? - Yes. Why, is that so inconceivable?
jokes.setup: Jerry expresses disbelief that someone would threaten suicide over George
jokes.punchline: George's genuine offense at Jerry's shock
jokes.explanation: Good character dynamics but relatively predictable for Jerry-George interaction.

jokes.text: Gotta kind of envy that. - You know, you've been developing quite the acid tongue lately.
jokes.setup: George makes a cutting remark about someone never seeing Jerry perform
jokes.punchline: Jerry's specific 'acid tongue' description calls out George's recent meanness
jokes.explanation: 'Acid tongue' is a great specific phrase that elevates the exchange.

jokes.text: If it's all-time, there's no contest. It begins and ends with Brezhnev.
jokes.setup: Discussion of ugliest world leaders
jokes.punchline: Jerry's absolute certainty about Brezhnev's unattractiveness
jokes.explanation: Solid delivery but relies on audience knowledge of political figures.

jokes.text: I got news for you. Golda Meir could make them all run up a tree.
jokes.setup: The men are debating ugly male leaders
jokes.punchline: Elaine introduces a woman and uses the vivid 'run up a tree' imagery
jokes.explanation: Great escalation with vivid animal imagery. Elaine's entrance into the debate works well.

jokes.text: You know just because you two are homosexuals, so what? I mean, you should come out of the closet and be openly gay already.
jokes.setup: Kramer warns about eavesdropping girls
jokes.punchline: His 'solution' is to loudly declare Jerry and George gay for the eavesdroppers
jokes.explanation: Perfect Kramer logic - trying to help but making everything worse. Sets up the entire episode premise.

jokes.text: You know you'll always be the only man I'll ever love.
jokes.setup: Kramer wants them to play along with the gay charade
jokes.punchline: Jerry delivers an over-the-top romantic declaration
jokes.explanation: Good commitment to the bit, though somewhat predictable romantic parody.

jokes.text: I could just see you in Berlin in 1939 goose-stepping past me. 'Come on, Jerry. Go along. Go along.'
jokes.setup: George refuses to play along with Kramer's scheme
jokes.punchline: Jerry makes an absurd Nazi comparison for not going along with a joke
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation - comparing not playing along with a gay joke to Nazi compliance. Dark and absurd.

jokes.text: Yeah, because I'm single, I'm thin, and I'm neat. - And you get along well with women. - I guess that leaves me in the clear.
jokes.setup: Jerry lists stereotypical 'gay' traits he possesses
jokes.punchline: George's self-deprecating admission that he doesn't get along with women
jokes.explanation: Excellent character work - George's relationship issues become his 'proof' of heterosexuality.

jokes.text: Buck Naked? - Yeah. How did you know that? - You told me that already. Like two months ago.
jokes.setup: Kramer excitedly shares his 'new' porn name idea
jokes.punchline: Jerry reveals Kramer already told him this obvious pun months ago
jokes.explanation: Good character beat for Kramer's repetitive nature, though the pun itself is obvious.

jokes.text: How you gonna get out of that one? - I don't know. I guess I have to wait for her to die.
jokes.setup: George received an ugly shirt from his girlfriend
jokes.punchline: His 'solution' is waiting for her death rather than hurting her feelings
jokes.explanation: Perfect George - his cowardice taken to absurd extremes. Dark humor delivered with casual resignation.

jokes.text: It always sounds so high and whiny.
jokes.setup: Jerry complains about hearing his voice on tape
jokes.punchline: The irony that his actual voice IS high and whiny
jokes.explanation: Meta-humor about Jerry's actual voice, but relatively common observation.

jokes.text: Jerry, did you wash this pear? - Yeah, I washed it. - It looks like it hasn't been washed. - So wash it.
jokes.setup: George questions Jerry's fruit-washing during an interview
jokes.punchline: Jerry's curt dismissal treats George like a nagging spouse
jokes.explanation: Sets up the 'old married couple' dynamic that gets quoted in the article. Good domestic tension.

jokes.text: You hear the way he talks to me?
jokes.setup: George appeals to the reporter about Jerry's treatment
jokes.punchline: He sounds like a spouse complaining about their marriage
jokes.explanation: Continues the domestic partner dynamic. George's wounded tone sells it.

jokes.text: You should hear how my boyfriend talks to me.
jokes.setup: Reporter responds to George's complaint about Jerry
jokes.punchline: She thinks George is complaining as Jerry's boyfriend
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection - the reporter's misunderstanding drives the plot forward efficiently.

jokes.text: Actually, we met in a gym locker room. - Yeah. - Actually, it was in gym class. I was trying to climb the ropes and Jerry was spotting me.
jokes.setup: Innocent question about how they met
jokes.punchline: Their real story sounds increasingly homoerotic when filtered through the reporter's assumption
jokes.explanation: Brilliant irony - their innocent friendship origin sounds romantic through the reporter's lens.

jokes.text: And I kept slipping and burning my thighs. And then finally I slipped, and I fell on Jerry's head. We've been close ever since.
jokes.setup: George continues the gym class story
jokes.punchline: The physical contact and 'close ever since' conclusion sounds like a meet-cute romance
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation of the accidental romance narrative. 'Close ever since' is the perfect closer.

jokes.text: Do your parents know? - Know what? - My parents? They don't know what's going on.
jokes.setup: Reporter asks if parents know about their relationship
jokes.punchline: George's response about parents not knowing 'what's going on' sounds like being in the closet
jokes.explanation: Good continuation of the misunderstanding. George's confused honesty works against him.

jokes.text: Not that there's anything wrong with that.
jokes.setup: Jerry tries to clarify they're not gay
jokes.punchline: The defensive disclaimer that becomes the episode's catchphrase
jokes.explanation: Iconic catchphrase that became cultural shorthand. Perfect political correctness anxiety of the 90s.

jokes.text: Look, you wanna have sex right now? Do you wanna have sex with me right now? Let's go. Come on! Let's go, baby! Come on!
jokes.setup: Jerry desperately tries to prove his heterosexuality
jokes.punchline: His aggressive sexual proposition is wildly inappropriate and unconvincing
jokes.explanation: Jerry's desperation is funny but the approach is a bit obvious. Good character panic moment.

jokes.text: Well, I don't wanna sit there naked all by myself.
jokes.setup: Kramer suggests taking a steam
jokes.punchline: His concern about being alone and naked sounds homoerotic given the context
jokes.explanation: Good Kramer obliviousness to how his words sound. Context makes innocent statement funny.

jokes.text: Because you know me, I walk into a room, problem solved.
jokes.setup: Kramer offers to talk to the reporter
jokes.punchline: His confidence is ironic since he created the problem in the first place
jokes.explanation: Perfect Kramer delusion. The irony is clear to audience but not to him.

jokes.text: Happy birthday. 'Faruba!' - Today's not my birthday. - Well, I beg to differ. - Well, I think I know when my birthday is. - Yeah, well, you'd think so, but you'd be wrong.
jokes.setup: Kramer wishes Jerry happy birthday on the wrong day
jokes.punchline: Kramer's absolute certainty that Jerry is wrong about his own birthday
jokes.explanation: Quintessential Kramer logic - being wrong with complete confidence. The escalation is perfect.

jokes.text: Maybe he's right. Maybe it is my birthday.
jokes.setup: Kramer insists it's Jerry's birthday
jokes.punchline: Jerry starts to doubt his own knowledge of his birthday
jokes.explanation: Great follow-up showing how Kramer's confidence can make anyone doubt reality.

jokes.text: I could hear you on the other line. - What are you talking about? - I heard what you said: 'Sharon, are you there?'
jokes.setup: Jerry thought he was secretly talking to George
jokes.punchline: The reporter heard his conversation confirming they were 'fooling' her about being straight
jokes.explanation: Good plot mechanism - technology betrays them. The mounting evidence builds nicely.

jokes.text: Not that there's anything wrong with that. - Not at all.
jokes.setup: Another instance of the gay rumors
jokes.punchline: Both characters now reflexively add the disclaimer
jokes.explanation: Running gag building momentum. The automatic response shows how defensive they've become.

jokes.text: Guys and Dolls? Isn't that a lavish Broadway musical?
jokes.setup: Elaine gives Jerry theater tickets
jokes.punchline: George's suspicious tone treats Broadway as evidence of homosexuality
jokes.explanation: Good paranoia beat. George now sees gay implications in everything.

jokes.text: It's Guys and Dolls, not Guys and Guys.
jokes.setup: George questions the musical's title
jokes.punchline: Jerry clarifies the heterosexual nature through wordplay
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective wordplay. Economical and clear.

jokes.text: The Collected Works of Bette Midler.
jokes.setup: Elaine gives Jerry a birthday gift
jokes.punchline: The gift choice seems to confirm gay stereotypes
jokes.explanation: Relies on stereotype about gay men and Bette Midler. Functional but not particularly clever.

jokes.text: I've been outed. I wasn't even in.
jokes.setup: Jerry discovers the story went national
jokes.punchline: The paradox of being outed from a closet you were never in
jokes.explanation: Brilliant wordplay and concept. Perfect economy - the entire situation in six words. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Seinfeld and Costanza bicker over the cleanliness of a piece of fruit like an old married couple.
jokes.setup: Jerry reads the newspaper article
jokes.punchline: Their earlier pear argument is recontextualized as marital bickering
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback showing how innocent interactions can be reinterpreted.

jokes.text: I told you that pear was washed.
jokes.setup: Reading about their 'marital bickering'
jokes.punchline: Jerry immediately proves the point by continuing to argue about the pear
jokes.explanation: Perfect irony - he proves the article's point immediately after reading it.

jokes.text: Oh, my God. Ma?
jokes.setup: George answers the phone expecting Jerry
jokes.punchline: It's Jerry's mother calling about the gay rumors
jokes.explanation: Good shock moment. George's horror at having to face Jerry's mother works well.

jokes.text: It was those culottes you made him wear when he was 5. - They weren't culottes. They were shorts. - They were culottes! You bought them in the girls' department.
jokes.setup: Jerry's parents discuss his homosexuality
jokes.punchline: Frank blames Helen's childhood clothing choices
jokes.explanation: Great parent dynamics. Frank's specific blame and Helen's defensive response feel authentic.

jokes.text: It looked like he was wearing a skirt, for crying out loud.
jokes.setup: Frank continues blaming Helen's clothing choice
jokes.punchline: His specific image of young Jerry in a skirt
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of Frank's theory. The visual is vivid and specific.

jokes.text: I fell right off the toilet.
jokes.setup: Helen describes reading the shocking news
jokes.punchline: The undignified image of falling off the toilet from surprise
jokes.explanation: Good physical comedy image. Universal experience of being shocked while in bathroom.

jokes.text: Maybe you're making porno films. - Yeah. I'm Buck Naked.
jokes.setup: Helen imagines Jerry's secret life
jokes.punchline: Jerry sarcastically references Kramer's porn name
jokes.explanation: Good callback and escalation. Jerry's sarcasm to his mother is character-appropriate.

jokes.text: Six-thirty, Scott. Time for your sponge bath.
jokes.setup: George is watching what appears to be a soap opera
jokes.punchline: The homoerotic subtext of the TV show George is watching
jokes.explanation: Visual gag about stereotypical 'gay' TV content. Somewhat obvious but fits the episode's theme.

jokes.text: I want you to know that it took a lot of guts to come out how you did and that you've inspired me to do the same, even though that may mean a discharge from the service.
jokes.setup: Jerry and George are recognized in public
jokes.punchline: A soldier credits them with inspiring his own coming out
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing real-world consequences. The military context adds weight.

jokes.text: If you boys cannot control yourselves, then I'm afraid I'm going to have to ask you to leave.
jokes.setup: Jerry and George argue loudly in a restaurant
jokes.punchline: The maitre d' treats their argument as a lovers' quarrel
jokes.explanation: Good public humiliation moment. The formal tone makes it funnier.

jokes.text: I'm out, baby! I'm out!
jokes.setup: George realizes his girlfriend will think he's gay
jokes.punchline: His celebration of being 'freed' from the relationship
jokes.explanation: Perfect George - finding the silver lining in disaster. His joy at escaping the relationship is very him.

jokes.text: I'm gay. I'm a gay man. I'm very, very gay. Extraordinarily gay. Steeped in gayness.
jokes.setup: George tries to convince his girlfriend he's gay
jokes.punchline: His over-the-top escalation of gay declarations
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation. 'Steeped in gayness' is a particularly funny phrase. George's desperation is palpable.

jokes.text: You remind me of Lois Lane.
jokes.setup: Jerry and Sharon are getting romantic
jokes.punchline: Jerry's nerdy reference at an intimate moment
jokes.explanation: Good character moment - Jerry's comic book reference during romance is very him.

jokes.text: I leave you alone for two seconds, and this is what you do? I trusted you!
jokes.setup: George catches Jerry with the female reporter
jokes.punchline: His jealous boyfriend act is over-the-top and inappropriate
jokes.explanation: Great escalation of the misunderstanding. George's committed performance as the betrayed lover is excellent.

jokes.text: My name's Buck Naked. I'm a porno actor.
jokes.setup: George desperately tries new lies to explain the situation
jokes.punchline: He appropriates Kramer's porn star identity
jokes.explanation: Good callback and escalation. George's escalating lies get more desperate and absurd.

jokes.text: I am not gay. I am, however, thin, single and neat.
jokes.setup: Jerry's closing monologue addresses the stereotypes
jokes.punchline: He acknowledges fitting the stereotypical traits
jokes.explanation: Good closing that ties back to the episode's themes about stereotypes and assumptions.

jokes.text: You know, Joe, I enjoy being gay with you, but it's about time you got in shape, tucked your shirt in and lost the wife.
jokes.setup: Jerry imagines discrimination within the gay community
jokes.punchline: The absurd suggestion that sloppy married gay men face discrimination
jokes.explanation: Creative extension of the stereotype theme. 'I enjoy being gay with you' is a particularly funny phrase.

jokes.text: Yeah. I got a feeling he's a little... [vacuum gesture]
jokes.setup: Jerry suggests people should vacuum instead of gossiping
jokes.punchline: The visual of making a vacuum gesture instead of the typical 'gay' gesture
jokes.explanation: Clever visual punchline that subverts expectations. The gesture substitution is creative.

tmdb_overview: Elaine's prank causes a reporter to misunderstand Jerry and George's relationship.
